questions.prompt: Why is `<button onClick={fn}>` better than `<div onClick={fn}>` for clickable elements?

choices.text: Native `<button>` includes built-in keyboard support (Tab focus, Enter/Space activation), screen reader announcement ('button, label'), default focus ring styling, automatic role='button' for assistive tech, and form-submission behavior in `<form>`. `<div onClick>` has none of this — keyboard users and screen reader users can't activate it without additional code (tabindex, key handlers, visual focus, role='button'). Add all that and you've reimplemented `<button>` worse. The first rule of a11y: use semantic HTML. Free accessibility from defaults beats manual reconstruction of the same behavior.

questions.explanation: Native button = keyboard, screen reader, focus, role, semantics — all free. Div onClick = manual reconstruction.

questions.prompt: Building a custom dropdown with custom styling. Accessibility approach?

choices.text: Add role='listbox' to a div and call it done.

Use Radix UI, React Aria, or shadcn/ui's accessible dropdown primitives. Building keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Home/End, type-ahead), focus management (move into menu on open, restore on close), ARIA (aria-expanded, aria-controls, aria-haspopup), and screen reader announcements correctly from scratch is genuinely hard — easy to get 80% there and miss critical edge cases that exclude users. The libraries get this right after years of refinement; you style on top. Strongly prefer using accessible primitives over hand-rolling the behavior.

questions.explanation: Use accessible primitives (Radix, React Aria) for complex widgets. Don't hand-roll keyboard/ARIA.
